coredata.dc:title: Manganese phthalocyanine and its graphene quantum dot conjugate: Synthesis, characterization electrochemistry, spectroelectrochemistry, electropolymerization, and electrochromism
coredata.dc:creator: AlMarzouq, Douaa
A. Majeed, Shereen
Budak, Özlem
Koca, Atıf
coredata.dc:description: Manganese phthalocyanine (MnPc) bearing peripherally substituted octacarbazole moieties was synthesized and characterized by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization spectrometry (MALDI), FT-IR, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The complex was then conjugated to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via π-π stacking and characterized by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction. Electrochemical performances of MnPc and its nanoconjugate were investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) and spectroelectrochemistry (SEC) to determine the influence of the carbazole substituents and graphene quantum dots on the redox mechanism of MnPc. Although the carbazole substituents did not significantly influence the reduction processes of MnPc, they induced MnPc coating onto the electrode surface due to cationic electropolymerization of the carbazole substituents. The composite comprising graphene quantum dots and MnPc exhibited redox responses that were comparable to those of MnPc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemistry
Electropolymerization
Spectroelectrochemistry
Phthalocyanine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Public Authority for Applied Education and Training (PAAET), Kuwait. This research and the findings achieved herein were supported by PAAET project number HS-19-03. The authors express their gratitude to the Kuwait University Research Administration-RSP unit general facilities of the Faculty of Science GFS GS 02/01, GS 03/01, GS 01/05), and to Dr. Abdullah Alhendal at the Chemistry Department – Kuwait University, for his support and access to facilities. The authors thank the Turkish Academy of Sciences (TUBA) for the financial support for electrochemical analysis.

The authors acknowledge the Public Authority for Applied Education and Training (PAAET), Kuwait. This research and the findings achieved herein were supported by PAAET project number HS-19-03. The authors express their gratitude to the Kuwait University Research Administration-RSP unit general facilities of the Faculty of Science GFS GS 02/01, GS 03/01, GS 01/05), and to Dr. Abdullah Alhendal at the Chemistry Department ? Kuwait University, for his support and access to facilities. The authors thank the Turkish Academy of Sciences (TUBA) for the financial support for electrochemical analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Normalized UV–Vis spectra of MnPc in various solvents, and (B) variation of the Q- band with F.
Representative TEM images of GQDs and MnPc-GQDs.
Cyclic and square wave voltammograms of MnPc in DCM/TBAP electrolyte with the GCE as the working electrode at varying scan rates.
Repetitive cyclic voltammograms of MnPc in DCM/TBAP electrolyte with the GCE working electrode at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1.
Cyclic and square wave voltammograms of MnPc-GQDs in DCM/TBAP electrolyte on the GCE working electrode at varying scan rates.

In-situ UV–Vis spectral changes of MnPc in DCM/TBAP electrolyte. a) Eapp = -0.20 V. b) Eapp = -0.85 V (inset: Eapp = 1.50 V). c) Eapp = 1.50 V. c) Chromaticity diagram (symbols represent the electro-generated species,□:[Cl1−-MnIIIPc−2],○ :[ Cl1−-MnIIPc−2]−1; Δ: [Cl1−-MnIPc−3]−2;☆: [Cl1−-MnIIIPc−1]+1 and their positions indicate their corresponding color.
Repetitive cyclic voltammograms of MnPc-GQDs in DCM/TBAP electrolyte on the GCE working electrode at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1.

Electrochromic measurements, a) T% changes under 1.50 V applied potential. b) Repetitive chronoamperometry (rCA), c) %T (at 700 nm) vs. time responses at Eapp = 0.00 V (time = 30 s) and Eapp 1.50 V (time = 30 s) in AN/TBAP electrolyte system, d) chromaticity diagram.
Synthetic route to MnPc. (i) DMF, CsF, 75–85 °C, 24 h; (ii) MnCl3, 1-pentanol, 130 °C, 24 h, N2 gas.
Conjugation of MnPc with GQDs. Color code: blue, nitrogen; gray, carbon; red, oxygen; green, chlorine; violet, manganese.
Electrochemical data for the complexes in DCM/TBAP solution. All potentials are given vs. Ag/AgCl.

head.ce:title: Manganese phthalocyanine and its graphene quantum dot conjugate: Synthesis, characterization electrochemistry, spectroelectrochemistry, electropolymerization, and electrochromism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Health, College of Health Sciences, The Public Authority of Applied Education and Training, P.O. Box 1428, Faiha 72853, Kuwait

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Public Authority of Applied Education and Training

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Health, College of Health Sciences, The Public Authority of Applied Education and Training, P.O. Box 1428, Faiha 72853, Kuwait

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Kuwait University, P.O. Box 5969, Safat 13060, Kuwait
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Kuwait University, P.O. Box 5969, Safat 13060, Kuwait

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Marmara University, Istanbul 34722, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Marmara University, Istanbul 34722, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Manganese phthalocyanine (MnPc) bearing peripherally substituted octacarbazole moieties was synthesized and characterized by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization spectrometry (MALDI), FT-IR, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The complex was then conjugated to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via π-π stacking and characterized by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction. Electrochemical performances of MnPc and its nanoconjugate were investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) and spectroelectrochemistry (SEC) to determine the influence of the carbazole substituents and graphene quantum dots on the redox mechanism of MnPc. Although the carbazole substituents did not significantly influence the reduction processes of MnPc, they induced MnPc coating onto the electrode surface due to cationic electropolymerization of the carbazole substituents. The composite comprising graphene quantum dots and MnPc exhibited redox responses that were comparable to those of MnPc.

ce:section.ce:para: The redox properties of the phthalocyanine (Pc) ring are well known and it has been established that the redox activity of Pc can be increased by complexation with redox active metal cations, such as Co2+, Fe3+, and Mn3+ [1–4]. In particular, manganese (III) phthalocyanines have attracted increasing attention as these complexes allow for two metal-based reductions and one metal-based oxidation in addition to the electron transfer reactions of the Pc ring [1,2,5–7]. Enhancing the redox activity of metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) has become a significant research topic for the purpose of broadening the utility of these complexes in various electrochemical applications, such as electrocatalysis, electrosensing and electrochromic [5,7]. Another preferred strategy for increasing the redox activity of Pc is the incorporation of various redox-active substituents on the Pc ring. In the literature, the influence of amine [8–10], thiophene [11,12], pyrrole [13], thiazole [14,15], and carbazole [16–18] substituents on the Pc ring have been well established. In our previous studies, we have described the redox activity of carbazole groups on the Pc ring and carbazole electropolymerization on the electrode surface [16–18].
The conjugation of phthalocyanine to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been extensively practiced to improve the electro- [19–21] and photophysical [22–24] properties of phthalocyanine complexes.

We previously have reported the phthalocyanine synthesis of different carbazole substituents [25] and different metals [26]. We also have reported the effect of conjugation of carbazole-containing phthalocyanines to GQDs on the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties [27].

In this study, MnPc comprising a redox active Mn3+ metal center and redox active octacarbazole-substituted units was synthesized and conjugated to GQDs to enhance the redox activity of the Pc ring, and their electrochemical properties were investigated to evaluate the influence of the carbazole groups on redox activity, electropolymerization, and electrochromic features.

Carbazole 1, anhydrous manganese chloride, and cesium fluoride salts were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. 5,6-Dichlorophthalonitrile 2 was purchased from TCI (Toshima, Japan). All solvents were obtained from local commercial suppliers. Compound 3 in this study was synthesized according to a literature procedure [25,26].

Electronic absorption spectra were measured with a Varian Cary 5 or Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. A Zeiss 1210 transmission electron microscope operated at a 100-kV acceleration voltage was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images.

Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) measurements were performed on a JEOL instrument with EDX attached to a Scanning Electron Microscope Model JSM IT100, functioning with integral SEM-EDX software in ordinary laboratory conditions.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected over the 2θ = 10–80° range, scanning at 0.010° min − 1 and 192 s per step. A zero-background silicon wafer slide was used and the measurements were performed using a Bruker® D8 Discover diffractometer, equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, under Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å). Elemental analyses were performed using an ElementarVario Micro Cube.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) and in situ spectroelectrochemical (SEC) measurements were performed using a potentiostat (GAMRY Instruments, Reference 600 Potentiostat/Galvanostat/ZRA) coupled with an Ocean Optics QE65000 diode array spectrophotometer according to a literature procedure [28]. In these experiments, a glassy carbon (GCE), Pt wire, and Ag/AgCl electrodes (containing saturated KCl aqueous electrolyte) were used as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. All measurements were performed in dichloromethane (DCM) / tetrabutylammonium perchloride (TBAP) electrolyte. In situ UV–Vis spectroelectrochemical and electrocolorimetric measurements were performed in a thin-layer quartz spectroelectrochemical cell with a three-electrode configuration using a Pt tulle working electrode, according to a literature procedure [28].

A solution of 4,5-di(9H-carbazol-9-yl)phthalonitrile 3 (1.00 g, 2.18 mmol), anhydrous MnCl3 (0.15 g, 0.82 mmol), 1-pentanol (10 mL), and two drops of 1,8-diazabicyclo-[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU) was stirred and heated at 130 °C for 24 h under N2. Upon cooling to room temperature the mixture was diluted with MeOH and filtered. The crude product was refluxed in methanol several times. The resultant precipitate was then purified via column chromatography using silica gel (MeOH then eluted gradually with CHCl3). The complex was characterized by infrared spectroscopy (IR), matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization spectrometry (MALDI), and UV–Vis spectroscopy. (See Scheme 1 ).

Elemental analysis: Calcd. for C128H72ClMnN16 + H2O: C, 79.15; H, 3.84; N, 11.54. Found: C, 79.32; H, 3.60; N, 11.65. FT-IR (KBr): υ, cm−1: 3050 (w, Ar-H); 1662 (m, CN); 1626 (m, CC); 1335 (m, CN). UV/Vis (DMF, 80.0 µM): λmax, nm (log ε) = 710 (3.92), 380 (4.44). MS (MALDI-TOF; HCCA): m/z (Calcd., Found) = (1888.99, 1888.47) [M−Cl]+; (1911.97, 1911.23) [M−Cl + Na]+.

Synthesis of GQDs and the nanoconjugate was achieved as described elsewhere [27]. Typically, 5 mg of GQDs dissolved in 2 mL DMF was added to 20.0 mg of the complex 4 in 10 mL of DMF. The resulting mixture was sonicated and stirred in the dark for 4 days. Methanol was used to precipitate and wash the products. The solid product was dried under vacuum overnight at 60 ⁰C.

The UV–Vis spectrum of MnPc indicated that the complex is in monomeric form (Fig. 1 and S3). As previously reported [25,26] for the UV–Vis absorptions of carbazole-containing phthalocyanine, a solvatochromic effect was likewise observed for the MnPc complex in various solvents, whereby the Q-band increases as the refractive index of the solvent (n) increases. The red shift of the Q-band of MnPc is presented in Fig. 1 . The Bayliss method [29] was used to analyze the solvents used, and the plot of the Q-band υmax vs. F = (n2-1)/(2n2 + 1) is shown in Fig. 1; the resulting correlation is defined by Eq. (1):
(1)
υ = - 9476.6 F + 15126
The MnPc complex was conjugated to GQDs via π-π stacking affording supramolecular nanohybrid MnPc-GQDs, as illustrated in Scheme 2 .

The TEM images of the nanoconjugate showed monodispersed particles, (Fig. 2 ), which was supported by the narrow Q-band in the UV–Vis spectrum of MnPc-GQDs (Fig.S4). However, some aggregates were observed in the TEM images of the GQDs and the nanoconjugate due to the π-π interaction among the GQDs in solid state [30–32].

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the GQDs alone and GQDs conjugated with the MnPc complex are exhibited in Fig. S5. The XRD spectrum of the GQDs shows a broad peak at 2θ = 18° corresponding to the (002) carbon Bragg reflection in the graphene layers [33]. The XRD pattern of MnPc alone contains peaks at 2θ = 18°, 20°, and 22° indicating a crystalline complex. These peaks overlap with the broad peak corresponds to GQDs; hence, no new peak was observed in the pattern of nanoconjugate after conjugation.

The elemental composition of the nanomaterials was determined by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) (Fig. S6). The GQDs were doped with nitrogen and sulfur, which were detected in the EDX spectra of both the GQDs alone and of the nanoconjugate.

Detailed voltammetric analyses of MnPc, and MnPc-GQDs were performed using CV and SWV in DCM/TBAP electrolyte on a GCE. Fundamental electrochemical parameters, including half-wave peak potentials (E 1/2), ratio of anodic and cathodic peak currents (I pa/I pc), and peak potential separations (ΔE p) of the redox couples are listed in Table 1 .

MnPc and MnPc-GQDs exhibited comparable redox responses. As shown in Fig. 3 , MnPc undergoes three one-electron reduction couples. The first couple was recorded at 0.070 V (Red.(1)), and assigned to the [Cl1−-MnIIIPc2−]/[ Cl1−-MnIIPc2−]1− process. The second reduction couple, Red.(2) at −0.73 V was assigned to the [Cl1−-MnIIPc2−]1−/[Cl1−-MnIPc2−]2− process. It is well documented that peak potential separation between the first and second metal-based reduction couples of MnPc should be less than 0.80 V [18,34]. Thus, the MnPc couples observed herein obey this rule. While these two reduction couples were recorded using CV, the third reduction couple, Red.(3) occurring at −1.15 V, could be observed with SWV, as shown in Fig. 4 b, and it was assigned to the Pc-based reduction of complex ([Cl1−-MnIPc2−]2−/[ MnIPc3−]3−). ΔE p, I pc vs. ν1/2, and I pa/I pc values at various scan rates indicated chemical quasi-reversibility and electrochemical reversibility of the Red.(1) and Red.(2) couples. The Red.(3) couple is chemically and electrochemically quasi-reversible. These voltammetric responses indicated that the Cl1− ligand is released after the third reduction process, which is the reason for the chemical quasi-reversible character of the third reduction process. During anodic potential scans, MnPc was coated on the surface of the working electrode due to cathodic electropolymerization of the carbazole substituents. As shown in Fig. 4, a large cathodic peak is observed at 1.40 V during the first CV scan due to the oxidation of the carbazole groups, which triggers cationic electropolymerization. Then, during consecutive CV scans, a new increasing couple (E pa = 1.25 and E pa = 1.15 V) was observed. This couple is observed at 1.01 V during the second CV cycle, shifting to 1.25 V by the fourth CV cycle. The peak current of this couple increases until the third CV cycle, and then decreases. These voltammetric responses correspond to the electropolymerization process and are in agreement with the responses of MPcs bearing carbazole substituents [16–18]. Peak assignments based on voltammetric analyses were supported by in-situ spectroelectrochemical measurements.

As shown in Fig. 5 , MnPc-GQDs exhibited similar CV and SWV responses as MnPc and all processes were recorded at comparable potentials. Reduction couples of MnPc-GQDs were observed at 0.071 V (Red.(1)), −0.72 V (Red.(2)), and −1.16 V (Red.(3)). As shown in Fig. 5 and Table 1, with respect to the ΔE p, I pc vs. ν1/2, and I pa/I pc values at various scan rates, it is evident that the GQDs increase the electrochemical reversibility of the reduction couples of MnPc. While the reduction processes of MnPc are quasi-reversible, all of these processes are fully reversible, as shown in Fig. 5. During the anodic potential scans, MnPc-GQDs gave rise to a similar electropolymerization process and was coated on the electrode surface.

As MnPc and the MnPc-GQDs composite displayed comparable voltammetric responses, their spectroelectrochemical responses were likewise similar. Therefore, in-situ spectral changes observed during in-situ SEC measurements of MnPc are given as a representative example in Fig. 6 . MnPc exhibited spectral changes characteristic of MnPc-type complexes [1,5,6,11,34]. In the neutral form, the B band of MnPc appears at 391 nm, the Q band at 761 nm, and the n-π* transition band at 676 nm. The characteristic band for the MnIII oxidation state of the central metal of MnPc was observed at 532 nm. The high-intensity bands at 272, 294, and 327 nm were attributed to the carbazole substituents. Due to the high intensity of the carbazole bands, the intensities of the Q band at 761 nm and the n-π* transition band at 676 are relatively low. During the first reduction reaction, the Q band shifts to 746 nm while the band at 532 nm disappears due to the reduction of [Cl1−-MnIIIPc2−] to [Cl1−-MnIIPc2−]1− species (Fig. 6a). Characteristic spectral changes for the reduction of [Cl1−-MnIIPc2−]1− to [Cl1−-MnIPc2−]1− were observed under an applied potential of −0.85 V, as shown in Fig. 6b. During this process, the Q band at 746 nm decreases in intensity, while the new band at 703 nm increases, indicative of the formation of the MnI oxidation state in the reduced species. Under an applied potential of 1.50 V, while the carbazole substituents of MnPc were electropolymerized on the electrode surface, [Cl1−-MnIIIPc2−] species are simultaneously oxidized to [Cl1−-MnIIIPc1−]1+ species. As shown in Fig. 6c, the decrease in the Q band intensity without a shift and the appearance of a new band at 890 nm support the Pc-based oxidation of MnPc. Moreover, formation of the film on the electrode surface indicates the oxidative cationic electropolymerization process at the same time. During the reduction reactions, the orange (x = 0.385 and y = 0.356) color of [Cl1−-MnIIIPc2−] becomes yellow (x = 0.376 and y = 0.383) and then green (x = 0.344 and y = 0.359), as shown in the chromaticity diagram in Fig. 6d. After the oxidation reaction, a dark orange (x = 0.402 and y = 0.384) color is observed for the oxidized species.

The spectroelectrochemical responses and color differences between the electrochemically generated species suggested that MnPc possibly exhibits electrochromic characteristics. To investigate the possibility of electrochromic utility, MnPc-GQDs was coated on an Indium thin oxide (ITO) electrode via electropolymerization and the electrochromic responses of the ITO/MnPc-GQDs electrode were analyzed. The repetitive CV responses displayed in Fig. 7 , indicated the successful coating of MnPc-GQDs via electropolymerization and the formation of an ITO/MnPc-GQDs electrochromic electrode. The red-colored film was clearly visible on the ITO surface with the naked eye.

For electrochromic applications, modified electrodes were fabricated employing the electropolymerization technique. The ITO substrate was modified with MnPc-GQDs to generate the ITO/MnPc-GQDs electrode, and its electrochromic properties were evaluated in acetonitrile (AN)/TBAP electrolyte. To investigate the electrochromic responses of the film, repetitive chronoamperometric (rCA) excitations were applied between 0.0 and 1.50 V at 30 s intervals, and the color changes and T% responses of the film were monitored. The UV–Vis spectra of the ITO/MnPc-GQDs film, depicted in Fig. 8 a, contain intense bands between 300 and 450 nm and a small Q band at 765 nm. When 1.50 V was applied to the ITO/MnPc-GQDs electrode, the %T of the region between 500 and 1000 nm changed drastically, resulting in a color change from red to green.

To induce the basic electrochromic behavior of the electrode, repetitive chronoamperometric (rCA) excitations were applied to the ITO/MnPc-GQDs electrode with 30 s time intervals between 0.0 V and 1.50 V. Changes in the current with time, T% responses at 850 nm with time, and color changes with respect to rCA excitations were recorded. As shown in Fig. 8b, stable anodic and cathodic current responses were observed during rCA, indicating coulombic stability of the electrode. Due to the repetitive oxidations and reductions of the ITO/MnPc-GQDs film, relatively uniform and rapid T% changes were observed at 850 nm with each rCA cycle, as shown in Fig. 8c. Response times for the color changes were 1.5 s and 2.3 s under 1.50 and 0.0 V rCA excitation, respectively, as derived from Fig. 8c. The colors of the neutral and oxidized films were observed as red (point ○x = 0.416 and y = 0.396) and green (point Δx = 0.336 and y = 0.409), respectively. The obtained electrochromic parameters demonstrate reasonable electrochemical responses of ITO/MnPc-GQDs, supporting their possible application in electrochromic devices. Unfortunately, the film started to dissolve in AN/TBAP electrolyte after the 6th rCA excitation. This drawback could be resolved by using gel or solid electrolyte in electrochromic devices.

The conjugation of octacarbazole-substituted phthalocyanine manganese complex (MnPc) with GQDs was accomplished. The complex and its nanoconjugates were characterized using spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. MnPc, bearing redox active carbazole substituents, exhibited extended metal-based redox processes at low potentials, which is advantageous for its potential utility as a functional material in electrochemical technologies. Moreover, carbazole substituents provided additional redox activity and induced the electropolymerization of the complex onto the electrode surface, which facilitated the electrode modification process. The electropolymerized film demonstrated improved electrochromic responses with rapid response times and excellent coulombic and optical stability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Public Authority for Applied Education and Training (PAAET), Kuwait. This research and the findings achieved herein were supported by PAAET project number HS-19-03. The authors express their gratitude to the Kuwait University Research Administration-RSP unit general facilities of the Faculty of Science GFS GS 02/01, GS 03/01, GS 01/05), and to Dr. Abdullah Alhendal at the Chemistry Department – Kuwait University, for his support and access to facilities. The authors thank the Turkish Academy of Sciences (TUBA) for the financial support for electrochemical analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ica.2021.120558.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemistry of manganese phthalocyanine in nonaqueous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Lever, P. Minor, J. Wilshire, Electrochemistry of manganese phthalocyanine in nonaqueous media, Inorganic Chemistry, 20 (1981) 2550-2553.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparative electrochemistry and electrocatalytic activities of cobalt, iron and manganese phthalocyanine complexes axially co-ordinated to mercaptopyridine self-assembled monolayer at gold electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.I. Ozoemena, T. Nyokong, Comparative electrochemistry and electrocatalytic activities of cobalt, iron and manganese phthalocyanine complexes axially co-ordinated to mercaptopyridine self-assembled monolayer at gold electrodes, Electrochimica acta, 51 (2006) 2669-2677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron-Donating or -Withdrawing Nature of Substituents Revealed by the Electrochemistry of Metal-Free Phthalocyanines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Li, X. Zhang, P. Zhu, D.K. Ng, N. Kobayashi, J. Jiang, Electron-donating or-withdrawing nature of substituents revealed by the electrochemistry of metal-free phthalocyanines, Inorganic chemistry, 45 (2006) 2327-2334.
A. Lever, P. Minor, Electrochemistry of main-group phthalocyanines, Inorganic Chemistry, 20 (1981) 4015-4017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroelectrochemical studies of manganese phthalocyanine thin films for applications in electrochromic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-L. Lin, C.-C. Lee, K.-C. Ho, Spectroelectrochemical studies of manganese phthalocyanine thin films for applications in electrochromic devices, Journal of electroanalytical Chemistry, 524 (2002) 81-89.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and electrochemical properties of benzyl-mercapto and dodecyl-mercapto tetrasubstituted manganese phthalocyanine complexes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Agboola, K.I. Ozoemena, P. Westbroek, T. Nyokong, Synthesis and electrochemical properties of benzyl-mercapto and dodecyl-mercapto tetrasubstituted manganese phthalocyanine complexes, Electrochimica acta, 52 (2007) 2520-2526.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An electrochemical sensor for the detection of pesticides based on the hybrid of manganese phthalocyanine and polyaniline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Akyüz, A. Koca, An electrochemical sensor for the detection of pesticides based on the hybrid of manganese phthalocyanine and polyaniline, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 283 (2019) 848-856.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electropolymerization of cobalt tetraamino-phthalocyanine at reduced graphene oxide for electrochemical determination of cysteine and hydrazine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Mani, S.-T. Huang, R. Devasenathipathy, T.C. Yang, Electropolymerization of cobalt tetraamino-phthalocyanine at reduced graphene oxide for electrochemical determination of cysteine and hydrazine, RSC advances, 6 (2016) 38463-38469.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Substituted phthalocyanines and their electropolymerization properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Bayrak, H.T. Akçay, Z. Bıyıklıoğlu, İ. Değirmencioğlu, Substituted phthalocyanines and their electropolymerization properties, Synthetic Metals, 220 (2016) 643-652.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, electrochemistry, spectroelectrochemistry and electropolymerization of metal-free and metallophthalocyanines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Çakır, H. Kantekin, Z. Bıyıklıoğlu, A. Koca, Synthesis, electrochemistry, spectroelectrochemistry and electropolymerization of metal-free and metallophthalocyanines, Polyhedron, 81 (2014) 525-533.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, electrochemical and electrocatalytic behaviour of thiophene-appended cobalt, manganese and zinc phthalocyanine complexes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Obirai, T. Nyokong, Synthesis, electrochemical and electrocatalytic behaviour of thiophene-appended cobalt, manganese and zinc phthalocyanine complexes, Electrochimica acta, 50 (2005) 5427-5434.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic activity of phthalocyanines bearing thiophenes for hydrogen production from water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ö.A. Osmanbaş, A. Koca, M. Kandaz, F. Karaca, Electrocatalytic activity of phthalocyanines bearing thiophenes for hydrogen production from water, International journal of hydrogen energy, 33 (2008) 3281-3288.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, spectral and electrochemical properties of a new family of pyrrole substituted cobalt, iron, manganese, nickel and zinc phthalocyanine complexes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Obirai, N.P. Rodrigues, F. Bedioui, T. Nyokong, Synthesis, spectral and electrochemical properties of a new family of pyrrole substituted cobalt, iron, manganese, nickel and zinc phthalocyanine complexes, Journal of Porphyrins and Phthalocyanines, 7 (2003) 508-520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metallo-phthalocyanines containing thiazole moieties: Synthesis, characterization, electrochemical and spectroelectrochemical properties and sensor applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Demir, H.Y. Yenilmez, A. Koca, Z.A.
